root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 1
root_sections.summary: This report quantifies Ethiopia’s import tax expenditures—revenue foregone from customs duties, exemptions, and preferential rates—over FY 2018/19 through FY 2020/21, providing sector‑by‑sector and policy‑objective analysis, published by the Tax Policy Directorate of the Ministry of Finance in September 2022.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 2
root_sections.summary: The document is an import‑tax‑expenditure report for Ethiopia covering fiscal years 2018/19 through 2020/21, produced by the IFS Centre for Tax Analysis in Developing Countries (TaxDev) in partnership with the Ethiopian Ministry of Finance Tax Policy Directorate, funded by UK aid, and © the Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 3
root_sections.summary: The report systematically presents the import‑tax expenditures for FY 2018/19 through 2020/21, offering an executive summary, introduction, policy rationale, definitions of the benchmark tax system, methodology for estimating tax expenditures, and detailed estimates based on import data.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 4
root_sections.summary: This report estimates Ethiopia's import tax expenditures for FY 2018/19–2020/21 by analyzing shipment-level microdata from the Ethiopian Customs Commission to quantify revenue foregone from customs duties, excise duties, VAT, and surtax.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 5
root_sections.summary: According to the FY 2018/19‑2020/21 import tax‑expenditure report, overall tax expenditures ranged from roughly 16 % of the import value of affected goods in FY 2020/21 (and FY 2019/20) to about 20 % in FY 2018/19, but in FY 2020/21 motor vehicles and animal/vegetable fats (mainly palm oil) together accounted for nearly 40 % of total tax expenditures with average rates around 50 % of import values, driven primarily by higher, inconsistently applied statutory surtax rates on vehicles and ad‑hoc exemptions for cooking‑oil imports to stabilise local prices, while tax exemptions for capital and investment goods and “second‑schedule” preferential customs and surtax rates remained relatively minor.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 6
root_sections.summary: The import tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19–2020/21 uses shipment‑level microdata from the Ethiopian Customs Commission to define a benchmark tax system and estimates federal import tax expenditures by comparing actual customs duty, excise duty, VAT and surtax collections against the benchmark, then aggregating the differences annually and by product‑service categories.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 7
root_sections.summary: The import tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19‑2020/21 outlines Ethiopia’s tax incentive regime—tax holidays, tax credits, and duty‑free import exemptions for capital/investment goods, construction materials, motor vehicles and spare parts (capped at 15 % of capital goods value) under Council of Ministers Regulation No. 270/2012—designed to stimulate investment, expand manufacturing capacity, strengthen domestic production, and promote exports for economic development.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 8
root_sections.summary: The import tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19–2020/21 indicates that Ethiopia’s tax incentives lack transparency, as businesses with no tax liability often fail to file returns, hindering comprehensive reporting and creating opportunities for rent‑seeking and corruption.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 9
root_sections.summary: It defines positive import tax expenditures as deviations from a benchmark tax system that reflects the standard customs‑duty rates set out in the relevant proclamations and regulations, with bilateral or multilateral agreements shaping the benchmark rates for specific countries of origin.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 10
root_sections.summary: The FY 2018/19‑2020/21 import tax expenditure report shows that Ethiopia’s sequential customs‑excise‑VAT‑surtax structure causes import tax incentives to raise subsequent domestic tax receipts, so the reported tax‑expenditure cost overstates the true net fiscal loss by attributing these indirect effects to the origin tax.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 11
root_sections.summary: Ethiopia's tax expenditure framework includes duty-free access for Sudanese goods under a bilateral agreement, duty-free imports for diplomats and certain international aid organizations, and excise taxes levied by Proclamation No. 1186/2020 on specific inelastic, luxury, or externality-generating imports.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 12
root_sections.summary: The tax‑expenditure report defines the import‑VAT benchmark as a 15 % tax applied to the CIF value plus customs and excise duties, covering basic food items and agricultural inputs, while providing exemptions under Proclamation 285/2002 and Vienna‑Convention‑based VAT‑free privileges for diplomats and certain international organisations.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 13
root_sections.summary: The benchmark surtax on imports is levied at a 10 % rate on the CIF value plus customs duty, excise tax and VAT for each importer, with exemptions for specific goods (e.g., fertilisers, petroleum, motor vehicles, aircraft and capital/investment goods) and for international organisations and their foreign personnel under the Vienna Conventions, of which only the capital‑ and investment‑goods exemption is counted as a tax expenditure.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 14
root_sections.summary: The report quantifies customs‑duty tax expenditures as the gap between the benchmark and actual duty rates applied to the CIF value (plus any indirect effects), and similarly computes excise‑tax expenditures as the difference between benchmark and actual excise rates multiplied by the CIF value adjusted for the applied customs duty rate, again incorporating indirect effects.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 15
key_entities: ORGANIZATION: Automated System for Customs Data
root_sections.summary: The report draws on merged shipment‑level customs microdata from Ethiopia’s ASYCUDA++ and eCMS systems for FY 2018/19‑2020/21—containing importer details, 8‑digit HS and CP codes, CIF values, duty rates, and paid customs duties, excise, VAT and surtax—though duplicate entries from the system transition may overstate tax expenditures by up to ETB 30.6 billion in a given fiscal year.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 16
root_sections.summary: In FY 2020/21 Ethiopia’s total tax expenditures reached ETB 120.7 billion—comprising forgone customs duty (ETB 38.9 bn), excise duty (ETB 18.8 bn), import VAT (ETB 44.6 bn) and surtax (ETB 18.4 bn)—representing a 54 % nominal year‑on‑year increase, far exceeding the 18 % rise in total import CIF value.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 17
root_sections.summary: The report quantifies Ethiopian import tax expenditures at ETB 68.7–99.3 billion (2.6–3.7% of GDP, approximately 20% of import value) for FY 2018/19–2020/21, with figures representing upper-bound estimates due to potential duplicate recording across two customs IT systems.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 18
root_sections.summary: From FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, capital/investment imports rose modestly in CIF value (≈ ETB 2.1 bn to 2.7 bn) while their tax expenditures surged dramatically in 2020/21 (≈ ETB 0.71 bn to 2.82 bn) driven by higher statutory excise tax rates on vehicles, non‑capital/second‑schedule imports dominated both CIF volumes (growing from ≈ ETB 462 bn to 554 bn) and associated tax expenditures (increasing from ≈ ETB 94.6 bn to 116.7 bn), and second‑schedule imports fell markedly in both value (≈ ETB 23.9 bn to 5.4 bn) and tax expenditure (≈ ETB 4.0 bn to 1.16 bn) over the period.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 19
root_sections.summary: The Ethiopian import tax‑expenditure data for FY 2018/19‑2020/21 shows a dramatic surge in tax foregone for animal or vegetable fats, climbing from ETB 3.5 billion in FY 2019/20 to ETB 21.8 billion in FY 2020/21 (over a three‑fold increase), while other commodity categories displayed varied changes over the period.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 20
root_sections.summary: The import tax‑expenditure data for FY 2018/19 through FY 2020/21 shows that animal or vegetable fats and prepared food & beverages (incl. sugar) consistently bore the highest tax‑expenditure share of import value—peaking at 50.1 % and 47.7 % in FY 2018/19 and declining to 48.1 % and 31.0 % by FY 2020/21—while all other commodity categories displayed substantially lower and relatively stable percentages, with notable modest fluctuations in textiles, base metals, electrical machinery, and pharmaceuticals.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 21
root_sections.summary: The FY 2018/19‑2020/21 import tax‑expenditure estimate is modestly higher than earlier projections, largely because the benchmark tax system is defined differently, and the report outlines two alternative benchmark approaches—one using standard rates from the Ethiopian Customs Commission and the other following the Ministry of Finance’s prior report.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 22
root_sections.summary: The import tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19–2020/21 estimates that using ECC standard rates as the benchmark—matching the headline rates for 86 % of customs duty, 98 % of excise, 84 % of VAT and 83 % of surtax shipments—yields overall tax expenditures of ETB 40.0 billion (1.5 % GDP) in 2018/19 and ETB 45.3 billion (1.3 % GDP) in 2019, substantially lower than estimates derived from higher benchmark rates.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 23
root_sections.summary: Ethiopia’s customs duty tax expenditures totaled roughly ETB 23 billion across FY 2018/19–2020/21, peaking at ETB 30 billion in FY 2018/19, with the bulk stemming from direct exemptions and rate reductions and indirect effects through surtax, VAT and excise, while exemptions alone accounted for about 71 % of total customs duty expenditures in FY 2020/21.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 24
root_sections.summary: In FY 2020/21 customs‑duty tax expenditures were about 35 % of imports, falling to roughly 5‑6 % of imports (≈30 % of total import tax expenditures) in FY 2019/20 and FY 2018/19, while capital and investment imports together with second‑schedule imports accounted for only about 2.8 % of the total customs‑duty tax expenditure in FY 2020/21.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 25
root_sections.summary: The table quantifies Ethiopia’s customs duty tax expenditures by three main categories (capital/investment, second schedule, and non‑capital/second schedule) for FY 2018/19‑2020/21, breaking out exemptions, rate reductions, and indirect effects (excise, VAT, surtax, higher‑order), with total expenditures varying from about 0.6 billion to 37.8 billion ETB across the period.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 26
root_sections.summary: The FY 2020/21 import tax‑expenditure report shows a pronounced surge in customs‑duty tax expenditures for animal or vegetable fats to ETB 10.68 billion (over 80 % of total expenditures) while other commodity groups such as prepared foods, mineral oils, and vehicles also generate substantial exemptions, rate reductions, and indirect effects across excise, VAT and surtax, indicating a major shift in revenue foregone compared with FY 2018/19 and FY 2019/20.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 27
root_sections.summary: The FY 2020/21 import‑tax expenditure report shows customs‑duty tax expenditures of ETB 798.82 million across commodity categories, while excise‑duty tax expenditures totaled ETB 18.8 billion (with direct effects comprising ETB 15.8 billion, or 84 % of the total), representing a 117 % rise over FY 2019/20 that was driven primarily by a surge in statutory excise‑tax liability on vehicle imports.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 28
root_sections.summary: Ethiopia’s excise‑duty tax expenditures rose from roughly 0.3 % of GDP (≈2 % of import CIF value, about 12 % of total import tax expenditures) in FY 2018/19 to 0.4 % of GDP (≈3.3 % of import CIF value, 15.6 % of total import tax expenditures) in FY 2020/21.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 29
key_entities: ORGANIZATION: Ministry of Planning and Development
root_sections.summary: Excise‑duty tax expenditures fell from roughly 12 % of imports, 8 % of GDP and 16 % of total trade‑tax expenditures in FY 2018/19 to about 4 % of imports, 2 % of GDP and 8 % of total trade‑tax expenditures in FY 2020/21, with capital and investment goods comprising only 9.2 % of excise‑duty expenditures in FY 2020/21 while the remaining ≈91 % (down from ≈99 % in FY 2018/19‑2019/20) stemmed from non‑capital, non‑second‑schedule shipments—driven by higher statutory excise tax rates on vehicles—and second‑schedule imports contributed negligible amounts.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 30
root_sections.summary: The excise duty tax expenditures grew from ETB 12.2 billion in FY 2018/19 to ETB 17.1 billion in FY 2020/21, with the non‑capital/second‑schedule category dominating the total and accounting for the bulk of both direct and indirect (VAT surtax and higher‑order) effects, while capital/investment expenditures rose sharply in FY 2020/21 and second‑schedule items remained negligible.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 31
root_sections.summary: The FY 2020/21 excise‑duty tax‑expenditure table shows that mineral oil & fuel (ETB 3,146.9 million) and prepared food & beverages (ETB 1,941.8 million) account for the largest expenditures, with the totals broken into direct excise, indirect VAT‑related, indirect surtax and higher‑order effects, while many commodity categories such as animal products, pharmaceuticals and fertiliser record zero tax expenditure.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 32
root_sections.summary: In FY 2020/21, tax expenditures on motor‑vehicle imports jumped to ETB 23 billion (0.5 % of GDP)—a three‑fold rise from FY 2019/20—driven mainly by higher statutory excise tax rates on used vehicles that failed to yield proportionate revenue, thus inflating excise‑duty and indirect customs‑duty expenditures.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 33
root_sections.summary: Ethiopia’s VAT tax expenditures grew from ETB 44 bn in FY 2018/19 and ETB 35 bn in FY 2019/20 to ETB 44.6 bn in FY 2020/21—94.7 % from direct effects and 5.3 % via surtax—reflecting a 26 % nominal increase, though the actual fiscal cost is likely lower because higher import VAT expenditures reduce domestic VAT deductions and therefore increase domestic VAT collection.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 34
root_sections.summary: The VAT expenditures as a share of imports, GDP, and total import tax expenditures declined from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, with over 98% of VAT expenditures in each year attributable to non-capital and non-second schedule imports.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 35
root_sections.summary: Between FY 2018/19 and FY 2020/21, total VAT expenditures grew from roughly ETB 43.7 billion to ETB 44.6 billion, with the non‑capital/second‑schedule category dominating (>95 % of the total) and direct effects consistently outweighing indirect effects, while in FY 2020/21 the leading commodity groups were machinery (other than electrical) at ETB 9.6 billion (21.5 %) and animal or vegetable fats at ETB 6.8 billion, together accounting for about half of the VAT expenditure.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 36
root_sections.summary: In FY 2020/21, Ethiopia’s VAT expenditures across 17 commodity categories totaled roughly ETB 42.8 billion, with “Other machinery” (≈ETB 9.6 bn), “Animal or vegetable fats” (≈ETB 6.8 bn) and “Electrical machinery” (≈ETB 4.1 bn) accounting for the bulk of the outlays, while “Leather and shoes” (≈ETB 22.5 mn) and “Paper and wood” (≈ETB 58 mn) contributed the least.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 37
root_sections.summary: From FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21 Ethiopia’s surtax import tax expenditures fell from ETB 13.5 billion (0.5 % of GDP, about 3 % of imports) to ETB 11 billion (0.4 % of GDP, 3.3 % of imports), while their share of total import‑tax expenditures rose from roughly 14 % to 15.3 %, with the surtax applied last in the border‑tax calculation and thus having no indirect effect on other tax collections.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 38
key_entities: ORGANIZATION: Ministry of Planning and Development
root_sections.summary: Ethiopia’s surtax expenditures fell from roughly 16 % of imports in FY 2018/19 to about 8 % in FY 2020/21, with capital/investment imports (1.0 %) and second‑schedule imports (1.9 %) together comprising only 2.9 % of total surtax in FY 2020/21, while the share of all other shipments rose to 97 %, up from 89 % in FY 2018/19.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 39
root_sections.summary: Ethiopia’s surtax expenditures grew from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, with non‑capital/second‑schedule items rising from about ETB 12 billion to ETB 17.9 billion, capital/investment surtaxes climbing from ETB 117 million to ETB 188 million, and the largest commodity‑group shares in FY 2020/21 being animal or vegetable fats (22.5 %) and machinery other than electrical (20.2 %).

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 40
root_sections.summary: The table lists the FY 2020/21 surtax‑expenditure amounts (direct effect) for 21 broad commodity categories in Ethiopia, ranging from zero for pharmaceuticals and fertiliser to over ETB 3.7 billion for “other machinery,” with the largest expenditures concentrated in machinery, animal‑or‑vegetable fats, and prepared food and beverages.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 41
root_sections.summary: Using shipment‑level Ethiopian Customs microdata and the revenue‑forgone method, the report estimates import tax expenditures at ETB 120.7 billion (21.3 % of import value, 2.8 % of GDP) for FY 2020/21, up from ETB 68.7–99.3 billion (≈20 % of import value, 2.6–3.7 % of GDP) in FY 2018/19 and ETB 76.4–78.3 billion (≈16 % of import value, 2.3 % of GDP) in FY 2019/20, indicating a substantial and growing fiscal cost that warrants regular scrutiny by the Ministry of Finance.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 42
root_sections.summary: The import‑tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19‑2020/21 is constrained by shipment‑level microdata that lack sufficient detail to diagnose underpayment, and augmenting these data with greater granularity, higher quality, earlier availability—plus higher‑quality microdata on domestic indirect taxes and comprehensive data on direct taxes—would substantially strengthen tax‑expenditure reporting.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 43
root_sections.summary: The segment defines negative tax expenditures as instances where the applied import tax rate exceeds the benchmark due to ad‑hoc rate changes, customs‑commission errors, or data‑recording mistakes, and explains that for the main estimates the observed higher rate is treated as a lower bound for the benchmark, thereby effectively excluding negative tax expenditures from the analysis.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 44
root_sections.summary: The report quantifies “negative tax expenditures” – the aggregate amount by which actual Customs, Excise, VAT and Surtax rates exceed the benchmark rates when the actual rate is not imposed as a lower bound – for fiscal years 2018/19 through 2020/21, presenting these as bar totals that include indirect effects calculated using the specified formula \(IE_c = (\tau_{bc}-\tau_{ac})\tau_{be}CIF + (\tau_{bc}-\tau_{ac})\tau_{bv}CIF\), based on Ministry of Revenues data under the assumption of no duplicate reporting across customs IT systems.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 45
root_sections.summary: It provides closed‑form formulas for the indirect effects of excise tax expenditures propagating through VAT and surtax, and of VAT expenditures through surtax, demonstrating that the sum of direct and indirect tax expenditures equals the difference between the benchmark and actual import tax paid on a shipment.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 46
root_sections.summary: The report quantifies customs duty, excise, VAT, and surtax tax expenditures for FY 2018/19‑2020/21 using both main and alternative estimation methods, while flagging possible duplicate records arising from the Ethiopian Customs Commission’s migration from the ASYCUDA++ system to eCMS between FY 2017/18 and FY 2019/20.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 47
root_sections.summary: The import tax on shipments recorded in eCMS alone equals 95 % of actual collections, while the combined eCMS and ASYCUDA++ reports reach 107 % of actual collections, indicating substantial double‑counting during the transition, with eCMS‑recorded shipments generating tax expenditures of ETB 68.7 billion (2.6 % of GDP) in FY 2018/19 and ETB 76.4 billion (2.3 % of GDP) in FY 2020/21.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 48
root_sections.summary: The document analyzes tax expenditures on imports reported through Ethiopia's eCMS and ASYCUDA IT systems over FY 2018/19–2020/21, revealing a declining trend in total tax expenditure (from ~140 to ~100 million Ethiopian Birr for eCMS), and proposes classifying these expenditures across 21 commodity subcategories using two-digit HS codes for detailed breakdown.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 49
root_sections.summary: This import tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19‑2020/21 defines a 21‑category commodity classification based on HS code chapters (1‑90) in Table E.1, which serves as the basis for detailed tax expenditure estimates presented in ETB millions in the subsequent supplementary tables.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 50
root_sections.summary: The import‑tax expenditure table for FY 2018/19‑2020/21 lists foregone import duties across 21 product categories (e.g., pharmaceuticals, chemicals, machinery, vehicles, textiles) with annual values and totals that quantify the revenue loss from tariff exemptions.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 51
root_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19‑2020/21 lists tax‑expenditure amounts for 17 product categories, with Vehicles (≈ 6,667 million), Base metals (≈ 4,568 million), Electrical machinery (≈ 3,532 million) and Textiles (≈ 2,419 million) accounting for the bulk of the roughly 25 billion‑unit total expenditure, while categories such as Other transport equipment (≈ 5 million) and Aircraft (negligible) contribute only minimal amounts.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 52
root_sections.summary: <output>The import tax expenditure table lists yearly tax‑expenditure amounts for each product category from FY 2018/19 through FY 2021/22, providing totals and showing that the highest expenditures are on mineral oil and fuel, vehicles, and prepared food and beverages, whereas several categories such as aircraft, pharmaceuticals, and base metals record no expenditure.</output>

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 53
root_sections.summary: The import‑tax expenditure data for FY 2018/19–2020/21 indicate that vehicles dominate customs tax forgone, amounting to roughly ETB 12.9 billion over the period, while the VAT expenditure table shows that mineral oil and fuel (≈ 5.42 bn ETB total), pharmaceuticals (≈ 3.92 bn ETB), and animal or vegetable fats (≈ 2.29 bn ETB) are the largest VAT‑expenditure commodity categories, with indirect surtax effects adding materially to several items.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 54
root_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19–2020/21 breaks down tax‑expenditure amounts by commodity sector, showing the largest subsidies for Other Machinery (~8.8 bn), Animal or Vegetable Fats (~6.8 bn), Base Metals (~2.5 bn), Electrical Machinery (~2.3 bn) and Mineral Oil (~3.4 bn), while considerably lower expenditures are recorded for sectors such as Leather & Shoes, Paper & Wood, and Aircraft.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 55
root_sections.summary: The import tax‑expenditure report for FY 2018/19–2020/21 shows that the largest tax foregone is concentrated in the machinery and electrical equipment categories (other machinery ≈ 4,420, electrical machinery ≈ 2,862), followed by base‑metal products (≈ 1,560–1,595), animal/vegetable fats (≈ 1,351–557) and vehicles (≈ 613), while pharmaceuticals and fertilizer record zero or minimal expenditure, indicating that industrial machinery and metal sectors dominate import‑tax forgone.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 56
root_sections.summary: The table quantifies import‑tax expenditure by commodity category for FY 2018/19‑2020/21, showing the highest tax outlays on electrical machinery, other machinery, vehicles and base metals, with a dash indicating zero expenditure, and the figures are derived from Ethiopian Customs Commission data under the assumption of no duplicate reporting.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 57
root_sections.summary: The import tax‑expenditure report for FY 2018/19–2020/21 lists the percentage share of total tax expenditures for each commodity across the three fiscal years, showing that electrical machinery, other machinery, vehicles, and mineral oil and fuel consistently account for the largest shares (e.g., other machinery peaks at 32.7 % in FY 2020/21), while the majority of other categories remain below 5 %.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 58
root_sections.summary: The import‑tax expenditure report shows that a few product groups—vehicles (up to 68.7 % in FY 2019/20), electrical machinery, other machinery and base metals—account for the overwhelming majority of tax‑expenditure shares, while all other sectors (e.g., pharmaceuticals, fertilisers, leather, paper, textiles, furniture) each contribute less than 5 % of the total.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 59
key_entities: ORGANIZATION: Ethiopian Revenue and Customs Authority
root_sections.summary: The import tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 quantifies the revenue foregone from import sur‑taxes and related investment incentives by applying Ethiopia’s regulatory framework and the IMF’s tax‑expenditure reporting methodology.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 60
root_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19–2020/21 quantifies the fiscal cost of import‑duty exemptions, preferences, and excise‑tax waivers granted under Ethiopia’s Customs Proclamation No. 859/2014, Industrial Park Proclamation No. 886/2015, and Excise Tax Proclamation No. 1186/2020, as compiled by the Ministry of Finance.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 61
root_sections.summary: The provided segment consists only of comma delimiters with no actual data, indicating an empty or placeholder dataset.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 62
root_sections.summary: The provided segment consists solely of placeholder commas and contains no substantive technical content.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 63
root_sections.summary: The table presents CIF import values and total expenditures for fiscal years 2018/19, 2019/20 and 2020/21 broken down by capital/investment, second schedule and non‑capital/second schedule categories, showing that non‑capital/second schedule entries dominate (e.g., imports of ~553,547 and expenditures of ~116,724 in 2020/21) and generally increase across the three years.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 64
root_sections.summary: The segment lists import values for fifteen commodity categories across the three fiscal years 2018/19, 2019/20 and 2020/21, showing a broadly upward trend in 2020/21—especially in vegetable products, animal‑or‑vegetable fats, textiles and base metals—while mineral oil & fuel and other mineral products declined in the same year.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 65
root_sections.summary: The segment provides a tabular breakdown of the percentage share of 17 commodity categories in total trade for fiscal years 2018/19, 2019/20 and 2020/21, showing year‑on‑year fluctuations such as the increase in animal products from 15.6% to 16.8%, the decline in prepared food and beverages from 47.7% to 31.0%, and varied trends across other sectors including textiles, base metals, and machinery.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 66
root_sections.summary: The data show a consistent increase in the reported metric across 2018/19, 2019/20 and 2020/21, with the overall report values rising from 99.3 to 120.7, eCMS only from 68.7 to 120.7, and ECC standard from 40.0 to 81.4, while the Ministry of Finance benchmarks are 73.9 for 2020 and 68.5 for 2022.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 67
root_sections.summary: The provided document segment consists solely of commasand ( line breaks) and contains no discernible technical content.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 68
root_sections.summary: The provided segment consists solely of repeated comma characters across multiple lines, indicating an empty or placeholder text with no substantive information.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 69
root_sections.summary: From 2018/19 to 2020/21, capital‑investment customs expenditure rose from €227.8 m to €466.6 m as indirect excise effects grew, the second‑schedule component fell from €2.17 bn to €1.15 bn, and non‑capital second‑schedule totals dropped from €27.45 bn to €21.93 bn, indicating a re‑weighting of customs revenue sources toward higher indirect‑tax contributions.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 70
root_sections.summary: The table breaks down each commodity category’s exemptions, rate reductions, indirect tax effects (excise, VAT, surtax), higher‑order impacts, and total customs expenditure, showing that animal or vegetable fats incur the highest total customs cost (≈10,680), followed by prepared food and beverages (≈1,386), whereas fertiliser registers zero across all fields.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 71
root_sections.summary: The “Others” entry aggregates six individual metric values (573.81, 54.07, 11.49, 94.18, 54.11, 11.16) that together sum to a total of 798.82, representing the combined contribution across those metrics.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 72
root_sections.summary: The segment contains only repeated comma delimiters with no actual data, indicating an empty or placeholder record.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 73

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 74
root_sections.summary: The segment presents the annual breakdown of excise‑tax effects into direct, indirect (via VAT and surtax) and higher‑order components for capital‑investment, second‑schedule and non‑capital expenditures over FY 2018/19‑2020/21, showing a pronounced rise in total excise tax for non‑capital second‑schedule items from €12.2 bn to €17.1 bn while capital‑investment excise tax remained modest but grew to €1.73 bn in FY 2020/21, with indirect effects contributing a small, consistent share.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 75
root_sections.summary: The table enumerates direct, indirect, higher‑order and total excise‑tax effects across commodity categories, revealing that vehicles, mineral oil and fuel, and prepared food and beverages bear the largest total excise‑tax liabilities while several categories such as animal products, pharmaceuticals, leather, and base metals show no excise‑tax impact.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 93

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 98

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 99

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 100
root_sections.summary: The data lists trade values for 17 product categories, with “Other machinery” (3,720.46) and “Electrical machinery” (3,255.18) dominating, followed by base metals (1,936.57), vehicles (1,009.97) and textiles (702.63), while pharmaceuticals (0.00), fertiliser (–) and aircraft (–) show zero or missing values.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 101
root_sections.summary: The 2018/19 data show that mineral oil and fuel accounted for the largest share of total import‑tax expenditure (11.4%) and the highest excise‑tax share (44.0%), while animal‑or‑vegetable fats contributed the greatest customs‑duty share (15.4%) and the highest combined share of customs duty, excise tax, VAT and surtax (8.3%), and prepared food and beverages carried a notable excise‑tax burden (24.0%).

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 102
root_sections.summary: The table lists the percentage contribution of 14 commodity categories to total imports across five fiscal years, showing that electrical machinery, other machinery, vehicles, and mineral oil and fuel consistently dominate the import composition (together accounting for roughly 15–33 % annually), while base metals, aircraft, other transport equipment, furniture, and several other sectors each represent less than 5 % in any given year.

root_sections.title: DOCUMENT SEGMENT 103
root_sections.summary: The data presents the percentage share of sixteen industrial product categories across five periods, showing that Vehicles, Electrical machinery, Other machinery, and Base metals collectively dominate the overall distribution, while sectors such as Pharmaceuticals, Fertiliser, Leather and shoes, Aircraft, and Other transport equipment contribute only minor shares.
